 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.1","title":"[FCP2-CONF] Golden Vectors + CDDL Pack","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] Golden Vectors + CDDL Pack

## Goal
Produce the canonical interoperability artifacts that prevent cross-implementation drift.

## Deliverables
- `FCP_CDDL_V2.cddl` covering NORMATIVE CBOR object schemas.
- Golden byte vectors for:
  - schema hash prefix + canonical CBOR
  - keyed ObjectId derivation for security object classes
  - signing-bytes canonicalization ("unsigned view" + deterministic CBOR) for at least:
    - ZoneCheckpoint / AuditHead / RevocationHead
    - and deterministic sorting of quorum signature arrays by node_id
  - COSE_Sign1 capability tokens (including chk_id/chk_seq + holder_node)
  - holder_proof signable bytes + verification
  - HPKE sealed boxes (ZoneKeyManifest + ObjectIdKey distribution) including required AAD binding
  - FCPS datagram envelope (session_id/seq/mac) + inner frame parsing (valid/invalid)
  - session transcript bytes + derived keys (including HelloRetry cookie + TransportLimits)

## Test/CI behavior
- Vectors are tested as exact bytes (no "close enough").
- A small CLI/test helper can regenerate vectors only with explicit intent.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Vectors run in CI and are stable.
- Changing canonicalization or hashing causes an obvious failing diff.

## Goal
Make decode/parse/verify surfaces resilient against malformed and adversarial inputs.

## Required fuzz targets (minimum)
- FCPS frame parsing
- FCPC frame parsing
- session handshake transcript verification
- capability token verification
- ZoneKeyManifest parsing + HPKE unseal behavior

## Corpora
- Include at least one "decode DoS" corpus designed to maximize decode CPU.
- Include corpora for common signature/CBOR malformations.

## Logging
- Fuzz failures must dump minimal reproducer input IDs and reason codes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fuzz targets are runnable via cargo (documented).
- Seed corpora committed.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.3","title":"[FCP2-CONF] Interop Test Suite (Handshake, Symbols, Tokens, Cross-Zone)","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] Interop Test Suite (Handshake, Symbols, Tokens, Cross-Zone)

## Goal
Verify that independent implementations can interoperate on the wire.

Even though the reference implementation is Rust, these tests should be structured so a second implementation (future Rust rewrite, Go/Python test client, etc.) can plug in.

## Scenarios (minimum)
### Sessions / handshake
- MeshSessionHello/Ack transcript verification:
  - deterministic transcript bytes
  - suite negotiation determinism
- HelloRetry cookie path:
  - responder issues cookie
  - initiator retries with cookie
- TransportLimits negotiation + enforcement:
  - max_datagram_bytes affects FCPS_DATAGRAM acceptance

### FCPS data plane
- FCPS_DATAGRAM envelope:
  - MAC computation and verification for Suite1 and Suite2
  - bounded replay window behavior
  - MTU enforcement
- Inner FCPS frame parsing:
  - header fields parsed exactly
  - symbol record parsing with strict bounds
- Per-symbol AEAD:
  - deterministic nonce derivation
  - AAD binding matches spec

### FCPC control plane
- FCPC frame parsing/ordering:
  - ordered delivery per direction
  - bounded replay window
  - k_ctx AEAD integrity (and confidentiality if enabled)

### Capability tokens + provable authority
- COSE_Sign1 encoding/verification
- grant_object_ids subset enforcement
- chk_id/chk_seq freshness binding
- holder_node + holder_proof verification

### Cross-zone enforcement
- deny cross-zone operations without required ApprovalToken
- allow with correct ApprovalToken evidence
- DecisionReceipt reason_code + evidence stability

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests are deterministic and can run in CI without a real tailnet.
- The test harness can ingest golden vectors as inputs and validate byte-for-byte expectations.

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.4","title":"[FCP2-CONF] System E2E Harness: Multi-Node Simulation + Structured Logs","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] System E2E Harness: Multi-Node Simulation + Structured Logs

## Goal
Provide end-to-end confidence that the platform works as a coherent system by building a deterministic, multi-node simulation harness.

## Key Idea
Run a multi-node simulation (in-process or multi-process) with deterministic identities and keys, so we can test:
- sessions
- symbol routing
- policy decisions
- receipts/audit
- repair loop behavior

## Deliverables

### 1. TestMeshNode Infrastructure

```rust
/// Deterministic mesh node for testing
pub struct TestMeshNode {
    pub node_id: NodeId,
    pub identity: MeshIdentity,
    pub sessions: SessionManager,
    pub object_store: InMemoryObjectStore,
    pub symbol_store: InMemorySymbolStore,
    /// Injected clock for deterministic time
    pub clock: MockClock,
}

impl TestMeshNode {
    /// Create node with deterministic seed
    pub fn new(seed: u64, node_index: u32) -> Self;
    
    /// Boot node and join mesh
    pub async fn start(&mut self) -> Result<(), HarnessError>;
    
    /// Graceful shutdown
    pub async fn stop(&mut self) -> Result<(), HarnessError>;
    
    /// Simulate crash (no graceful shutdown)
    pub fn crash(&mut self);
}
```

### 2. TestHarness Controller

```rust
/// Multi-node test harness
pub struct TestHarness {
    pub nodes: Vec<TestMeshNode>,
    pub network: SimulatedNetwork,
    pub clock: SharedMockClock,
    pub log_collector: LogCollector,
}

impl TestHarness {
    /// Create N-node mesh with deterministic keys
    pub fn new(node_count: usize, seed: u64) -> Self;
    
    /// Advance simulated time
    pub fn advance_time(&mut self, duration: Duration);
    
    /// Inject network partition between nodes
    pub fn partition(&mut self, isolated: &[NodeId]);
    
    /// Heal network partition
    pub fn heal_partition(&mut self);
    
    /// Inject packet loss
    pub fn set_packet_loss(&mut self, from: NodeId, to: NodeId, rate: f64);
    
    /// Get aggregated logs for analysis
    pub fn logs(&self) -> &[LogEntry];
    
    /// Wait for mesh convergence
    pub async fn wait_for_convergence(&mut self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<(), Timeout>;
}
```

### 3. TestConnector (Reference Implementation)

A minimal test connector that exercises all platform primitives:

```rust
/// Test connector for E2E validation
pub struct TestConnector {
    /// Echo operation (validates invoke/receipt)
    echo: EchoOperation,
    /// Counter state (validates state model)
    counter: CounterState,
    /// Event emitter (validates streaming)
    events: EventEmitter,
}

impl TestConnector {
    /// Operations:
    /// - echo(message) -> message (validates invoke path)
    /// - increment() -> counter (validates state)
    /// - subscribe() -> events (validates streaming)
    /// - simulate(op) -> preflight (validates simulate)
}
```

### 4. SimulatedNetwork

```rust
/// Network simulation with fault injection
pub struct SimulatedNetwork {
    /// Latency between node pairs (symmetric)
    latency_matrix: HashMap<(NodeId, NodeId), Duration>,
    /// Packet loss rate between node pairs
    loss_matrix: HashMap<(NodeId, NodeId), f64>,
    /// Partitioned node sets
    partitions: Vec<HashSet<NodeId>>,
    /// Message queue (ordered by delivery time)
    queue: BinaryHeap<PendingMessage>,
}
```

### 5. MockClock

```rust
/// Deterministic clock for testing
pub struct MockClock {
    /// Current simulated time
    now: Instant,
    /// Pending timers
    timers: BinaryHeap<Timer>,
}

impl MockClock {
    /// Advance to next timer or by duration
    pub fn advance(&mut self, duration: Duration);
    
    /// Advance to next pending timer
    pub fn advance_to_next_timer(&mut self) -> Option<Duration>;
}
```

## Logging Requirements

### Aggregated Log Format
All nodes emit to a shared LogCollector with fields:

```json
{
  "timestamp": "simulated-RFC3339",
  "real_time": "actual-RFC3339",
  "node_id": "node-0",
  "test_name": "test_partition_recovery",
  "phase": "execute",
  "correlation_id": "uuid",
  "event_type": "session_established|symbol_routed|operation_completed|denial|gossip_sync",
  "details": {...}
}
```

### Log Collection API

```rust
impl LogCollector {
    /// Filter logs by node
    pub fn for_node(&self, node: NodeId) -> Vec<LogEntry>;
    
    /// Filter logs by correlation_id
    pub fn for_correlation(&self, id: CorrelationId) -> Vec<LogEntry>;
    
    /// Find denials
    pub fn denials(&self) -> Vec<LogEntry>;
    
    /// Export as JSONL for analysis
    pub fn to_jsonl(&self) -> String;
}
```

## E2E Scenario Scripts

The harness enables running required scenarios from STANDARD (1n78.35):

| Scenario | Nodes | What it validates |
|----------|-------|-------------------|
| Happy path | 3 | install → invoke → receipt → audit |
| Default deny | 3 | missing cap → denial + DecisionReceipt |
| Revocation | 3 | issue → use → revoke → denial |
| Taint/approval | 3 | tainted input → denial → approval |
| Offline/repair | 3 | reduced availability → repair → recovery |
| Partition recovery | 3 | partition → divergence → heal → convergence |

## CI Integration

```yaml
e2e-harness:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    - run: cargo test -p fcp-e2e-harness --release -- --nocapture --test-threads=1
    - run: |
        # Extract log JSONL from test output
        ./scripts/extract_e2e_logs.sh > e2e-logs.jsonl
    - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
      if: always()
      with:
        name: e2e-harness-logs
        path: e2e-logs.jsonl
```

## Test Output Requirements

Each E2E scenario produces:
1. **Pass/Fail result** with assertion details
2. **Log JSONL** for all nodes throughout scenario
3. **Timing breakdown** per phase
4. **Coverage metrics** (which code paths exercised)

## Acceptance Criteria
- CI can run the E2E harness with `--nocapture` and produce usable logs
- All five required scenarios from STANDARD pass
- Partition/recovery scenario validates gossip convergence
- Logs include sufficient detail to debug failures without re-running locally
- Total E2E suite completes in <5 minutes on CI

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.5","title":"[FCP2-CONF] Mechanical Connector Compliance Runner (Static + Dynamic)","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] Mechanical Connector Compliance Runner (Static + Dynamic)

## Goal
Given a connector binary, mechanically verify it meets the FCP2 connector contract.

## Static checks
- `--manifest` output parses and validates strictly.
- Manifest versioning fields are present and sane:
  - `schema_version`, `min_mesh_version`, `min_protocol`, `interface_hash`
- Canonical IDs and schema versions.
- Capability declarations are explicit (required/optional/forbidden).
- Zone constraints (`home`, allowed_sources/targets, forbidden) are present.
- Sandbox profile + network constraints are within zone ceilings.

## Dynamic checks
Run the connector under the host/SDK harness and probe:
- standard methods (handshake/describe/introspect/capabilities/configure/invoke/health/shutdown)
- **optional** preflight: if connector exposes `simulate`, probe simulate for at least one operation and verify:
  - response is well-formed and deterministic
  - no side effects are observed in the harness (no receipts/intents, no state mutation beyond caches)
- capability gating (default deny)
- DecisionReceipt production on denials (stable reason_code + evidence)
- event subscription/replay behavior (if declared)
- no secret leakage in logs

## Acceptance Criteria
- Compliance runner can be used in CI to accept/reject a connector artifact.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.6","title":"[FCP2-CONF] FZPF v0.1 JSON Schema Pack (Policy Authoring + Validation)","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] FZPF v0.1 JSON Schema Pack (Policy Authoring + Validation)

## Goal
Make policy authoring **mechanical and verifiable** by shipping the **FZPF v0.1 JSON Schema** (Spec Appendix A) as an explicit artifact, with tests.

Even though FCP2’s canonical objects are deterministic CBOR, users and tools still need a **human-authorable** representation for zone/policy definitions.

## Scope
- Define/ship `FZPF_v0.1.schema.json` (or equivalent) in-repo.
- Provide golden example documents that cover the important policy shapes.
- Validate that policy JSON inputs can be validated deterministically and fail-closed.

## Design Notes
- This is **V2-only**. Any older “hub” concepts are out of scope.
- FZPF should be aligned to the V2 policy object model:
  - ZoneDefinitionObject
  - ZonePolicyObject
  - RoleObject / RoleAssignment
  - ApprovalToken constraints (JSON Pointer only)
  - ZoneTransportPolicy and port-gating fields

## Deliverables
- JSON schema file for FZPF (v0.1).
- Example policy documents:
  - minimal zone definition + policy
  - role bundles + assignment + attenuation
  - transport restrictions (DERP/Funnel forbidden)
  - revocation/checkpoint freshness policy (Strict/Warn/BestEffort)
  - taint/approval constraints (elevation + declassification)
- Unit tests:
  - validate example docs against schema

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.22","title":"[FCP2] CLI: install/doctor/explain/bench/audit/repair","description":"# [FCP2] CLI: install/doctor/explain/bench/audit/repair

## Goal
Ship an operator/dev CLI that makes the system operable **without disabling security**.

Key principle: if you can't explain a denial or quantify offline availability, the system isn't finished.

## Command set (MVP)
### Safety + debugging
- `fcp doctor --zone <zone>`
  - report checkpoint freshness, revocation freshness, degraded-mode state
  - report transport policy state (DERP/Funnel allowed?)
- `fcp explain --request <objectid>`
  - render DecisionReceipt evidence and reason_code
  - include pointers to relevant objects (token, grants, checkpoint, approvals)

### Storage + availability
- `fcp repair status --zone <zone>`
  - report coverage metrics (bps), distinct nodes, max concentration
  - report pending/last repair actions
- `fcp audit tail --zone <zone>`
  - tail AuditEvent stream with trace correlation

### Install + verification
- `fcp install <connector>`
  - verify supply chain chain and policy requirements
  - install/mirror connector into mesh store

### Performance
- `fcp bench ...`
  - canonical CBOR encode/decode
  - ObjectId derivation
  - token verify
  - FCPS parse+verify
  - RaptorQ decode
  - invoke-local and invoke-mesh

## Output contracts
- All commands MUST support a machine-readable `--json` output mode.
- Errors MUST be stable FCP-XXXX codes with retry semantics.
- Commands MUST NOT print secrets (including auth headers, tokens, and raw secret material).

## Testing
- Snapshot tests for `--json` output schemas.
- E2E tests in the deterministic harness:
  - `fcp install` + invoke
  - `fcp explain` on a denial
  - `fcp doctor` showing stale/fresh transitions
  - `fcp repair status` showing coverage changes

## Acceptance Criteria
- CLI commands operate purely from mesh objects and verifiable receipts.
- A denial can be diagnosed from DecisionReceipt + CLI output alone.

## Implementation note
The concrete CLI implementation work is tracked in the child bead `flywheel_connectors-iqrb`.

#### CostEstimate Scenarios
```rust
#[test]
fn test_cost_estimate_deterministic() {
    // Given the same inputs, cost estimate must be identical
    let req = SimulateRequest { estimate_cost: true, ... };
    let r1 = connector.simulate(req.clone()).await?;
    let r2 = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    assert_eq\!(r1.estimated_cost, r2.estimated_cost);
}

#[test]
fn test_cost_estimate_no_pricing_tables() {
    // CostEstimate must NOT contain CurrencyCost unless connector
    // has explicit, stable currency basis
    let resp = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    if let Some(cost) = resp.estimated_cost {
        // api_credits, estimated_duration_ms, estimated_bytes are OK
        // currency must be None or explicitly stable
        assert\!(cost.currency.is_none() || cost.currency_is_stable);
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_availability_check_rate_limit_headroom() {
    // ResourceAvailability must include rate limit signals when available
    let req = SimulateRequest { check_availability: true, ... };
    let resp = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    if let Some(avail) = resp.availability {
        // Should report remaining/reset if the connector tracks rate limits
        assert\!(avail.rate_limit_remaining.is_some() || avail.no_rate_limit);
    }
}
```

fn test_simulate_timeout_no_secret_leak() {
    // Ensure timeout errors do not include request data

}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-r6ty","title":"fcp.terraform — Infrastructure as Code Connector","description":"# fcp.terraform - Infrastructure as Code Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Deliver the FCP2 Terraform connector epic plan for IaC workflows with strict sandboxing, plan/diff safety, and policy-gated apply (dangerous).

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Strategic Context

### Value Assessment
**Strategic Value Score: 80/100** — Important Tier 3 connector (Infrastructure family).

**Why Critical**: Terraform is the **dominant Infrastructure-as-Code tool**, managing cloud resources across AWS, GCP, Azure, and 1000+ providers. Enables agents to provision infrastructure, review plans, and automate cloud operations. Essential for autonomous DevOps.

### Priority Matrix Position
- **Category**: Specialized Investment (Infrastructure + Safety Critical)
- **Build Phase**: Phase 3 Enterprise (weeks 9-12)

### Pulumi Comparison
- **Terraform**: HCL-based, mature ecosystem, state management
- **Pulumi**: General-purpose languages, same cloud targets
- Both needed for full IaC coverage

---

## Agent Capabilities (Concrete Use Cases)

### Plan & Apply
- Generate infrastructure plans
- Review change diffs
- Apply approved changes
- Destroy resources (controlled)

### State Management
- Read current state
- Import existing resources
- State drift detection
- Resource inventory

### Workspace Management
- List workspaces
- Switch workspaces
- Lock/unlock state

### Examples of Agent Workflows
```
"What will this change affect?"
"Show me the current infrastructure state"
"Apply the approved changes"
"What resources exist in production?"
"Has any infrastructure drifted?"
"Import this existing S3 bucket"
"Create a new staging workspace"
```

---

## Flywheel Ecosystem Synergies

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-r6ty.1","title":"fcp.terraform: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)","description":"# fcp.terraform: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)

## Goal
Define the Terraform connector’s **mechanical manifest contract** under FCP2.

Terraform is safety-critical; the manifest must make dangerous operations mechanically obvious.

## Required contents
- Manifest archetypes (closed set):
  - `operational` (CLI invocation; plan/apply/state)
- Operation list + schema IDs.
- Capability families + per-operation required caps.
- NetworkConstraints:
  - default deny
  - explicitly allow only the minimal outbound hosts required for the configured backends/providers
  - optionally require “offline mode” for plan-only environments
- Sandbox profile:
  - strict filesystem allowlist
  - explicit binary allowlist (terraform + minimal helpers)
- State model declaration:
  - plan artifact references + hashes (if persisted)
  - drift checkpoints (if persisted)

## Acceptance criteria
- Another engineer can implement `--manifest` from this bead alone.
- Static compliance can reject:
  - overly-broad egress
  - unsafe filesystem profiles

  - `ha.entity.list`

  - redaction of sensitive attributes

## Acceptance criteria

## Strategic Context

**Strategic Value Score: 85/100** — Important Tier 2 connector (Observability family).

## V2 Specification Compliance (NORMATIVE)

### ConnectorArchetype
```rust
archetypes: vec\![
    ConnectorArchetype::Operational,  // Issue management, alerts
    ConnectorArchetype::Streaming,    // Webhook events
]
```
- **Operational**: Issue CRUD, alert configuration, release tracking
- **Streaming**: Webhooks for new issues, regressions, alerts

### ConnectorStateModel
```rust
state_model: ConnectorStateModel::SingletonWriter
```
- **SingletonWriter**: Webhook deduplication, issue sync state
- State tracked: `{ processed_events: HashSet<EventId>, sync_cursors: HashMap<ProjectId, Cursor> }`
- Lease required for writer fencing

### ConnectorFormat
```rust
format: ConnectorFormat::Wasi
```
- **WASI recommended**: Handles API tokens
- Memory isolation for credential protection
- Capability-gated hostcalls

### NetworkConstraints (NORMATIVE)
```rust
NetworkConstraints {
    // Sentry API endpoints
    host_allow: vec\![
        "sentry.io".into(),
        "*.sentry.io".into(),
        // Self-hosted: explicitly configured
    ],
    port_allow: vec\![443],
    deny_localhost: true,
    deny_private_ranges: true,  // Override for self-hosted
    deny_tailnet_ranges: true,
    deny_ip_literals: true,
    require_sni: true,
    require_host_canonicalization: true,
}
```

### TaintFlags (NORMATIVE)
```rust
// Stack traces may contain sensitive data
stack_trace.provenance.taint = TaintFlags::EXTERNAL_INPUT;

// User context in errors is PII
user_context.provenance.taint = TaintFlags::USER_SUPPLIED;

// Webhook payloads are external
webhook_event.provenance.taint = TaintFlags::EXTERNAL_INPUT;

// URLs in breadcrumbs are unverified
if breadcrumb.contains_urls() {
    breadcrumb.provenance.taint |= TaintFlags::UNVERIFIED_LINK;
}
```

| Operation | SafetyTier | Rationale |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| `sentry.issues.read` | Safe | Read-only |
| `sentry.events.read` | Safe | Read-only |
| `sentry.projects.read` | Safe | Metadata |
| `sentry.issues.update` | Risky | Status changes |
| `sentry.issues.assign` | Risky | Routing changes |
| `sentry.alerts.manage` | Dangerous | Alert configuration |
| `sentry.issues.delete` | Dangerous | Irreversible |

## Goal
Validate `fcp-host` core behaviors (supervision, sandboxing, policy enforcement, logs) with deterministic tests and mocks.

## Scope
Unit tests for the Gateway/Orchestrator implementation.

## Test Categories

### Connector Supervisor
- [ ] Connector process spawning
- [ ] Process termination handling
- [ ] Health monitoring (heartbeat success/failure)
- [ ] Restart policy enforcement (max_restarts, backoff)
- [ ] Resource limit enforcement (memory, CPU)
- [ ] Crash detection and recovery
- [ ] Graceful shutdown signaling

#### Golden Vectors
```rust
// tests/vectors/host/supervisor_restart_policy.json
{
  "policy": {
    "max_restarts": 3,
    "backoff_base_ms": 100,
    "backoff_max_ms": 10000,
    "window_seconds": 60
  },
  "crashes": [
    {"at_ms": 0, "restart": true, "delay_ms": 100},
    {"at_ms": 200, "restart": true, "delay_ms": 200},
    {"at_ms": 600, "restart": true, "delay_ms": 400},
    {"at_ms": 1500, "restart": false, "reason": "max_restarts_exceeded"}
  ]
}
```

### IPC Hub
- [ ] Message routing correctness
- [ ] Request/response correlation
- [ ] Timeout handling (configurable per operation)
- [ ] Event fan-out to multiple subscribers
- [ ] Connection lifecycle (connect, disconnect, reconnect)
- [ ] Backpressure handling

#### IPC Test Scenarios
```rust
#[test]
fn test_request_response_correlation() {
    // Concurrent requests must be correlated correctly
    let hub = IpcHub::new();
    let req1 = hub.send_request("connector-a", Request { id: 1, ... }).await;
    let req2 = hub.send_request("connector-a", Request { id: 2, ... }).await;
    
    // Responses can arrive out of order
    hub.receive_response(Response { id: 2, ... });
    hub.receive_response(Response { id: 1, ... });
    
    // Each request gets correct response
    assert_eq\!(req1.await?.id, 1);
    assert_eq\!(req2.await?.id, 2);
}

#[test]
fn test_timeout_handling() {
    let hub = IpcHub::new_with_timeout(Duration::from_millis(100));
    let req = hub.send_request("connector-a", Request { ... });
    
    // No response within timeout
    let err = req.await.unwrap_err();
    assert\!(matches\!(err, IpcError::Timeout { operation_id, .. }));
}
```

### Zone Enforcer
- [ ] Zone configuration loading from ZoneDefinition
- [ ] Request zone validation (single-zone binding)
- [ ] Cross-zone policy enforcement (default deny)
- [ ] Data flow control (taint labels)
- [ ] Zone membership verification

### Capability Verifier
- [ ] COSE_Sign1 token parsing
- [ ] Ed25519 signature verification
- [ ] Capability scope checking (operation, connector)
- [ ] Constraint enforcement (rate limits, resource limits)
- [ ] Expiration handling (valid_from, valid_until)
- [ ] Grant object ID subset verification
- [ ] Revocation check integration

#### Capability Test Scenarios
```rust
// tests/vectors/host/capability_verification.json
[
  {
    "name": "valid_token",
    "token_hex": "d28443...",
    "expected": {"valid": true, "operations": ["invoke"]}
  },
  {
    "name": "expired_token",
    "token_hex": "d28443...",
    "expected": {"valid": false, "error": "FCP-2102"}
  },
  {
    "name": "invalid_signature",
    "token_hex": "d28443...",
    "expected": {"valid": false, "error": "FCP-2104"}
  },
  {
    "name": "revoked_grant",
    "token_hex": "d28443...",
    "revocation_seq": 42,
    "expected": {"valid": false, "error": "FCP-2105"}
  }
]
```

### Sandbox Manager
- [ ] Filesystem isolation setup (allowed paths, read-only mounts)
- [ ] Network filtering rules (NetworkConstraints)
- [ ] Configuration generation (TOML/JSON for jail)
- [ ] Resource cgroup configuration
- [ ] Credential injection (sealed secrets)

#### Sandbox Test Scenarios
```rust
#[test]
fn test_filesystem_isolation() {
    let sandbox = SandboxManager::new();
    let config = sandbox.configure(&ConnectorManifest {
        network_constraints: NetworkConstraints::Moderate,
        filesystem_access: vec\!["/data/connector"],
        ...
    });
    
    assert\!(config.allowed_paths.contains("/data/connector"));
    assert\!(\!config.allowed_paths.contains("/etc/passwd"));
    assert\!(config.read_only_paths.contains("/usr"));
}

#[test]
fn test_network_filtering_strict() {
    let config = sandbox.configure(&ConnectorManifest {
        network_constraints: NetworkConstraints::Strict,
        ...
    });
    
    // Strict = no raw sockets, DNS only to resolver
    assert\!(\!config.allow_raw_sockets);
    assert_eq\!(config.dns_servers, vec\!["127.0.0.53"]);
}
```

### Agent API
- [ ] HTTP endpoint routing (REST operations)
- [ ] WebSocket handling (streaming subscriptions)
- [ ] Request validation (schema verification)
- [ ] Response formatting (canonical JSON)
- [ ] Error handling (structured FCP errors)
- [ ] Rate limiting (per-principal)

### Connector Registry
- [ ] Manifest parsing (TOML, embedded)
- [ ] Signature verification (Ed25519 over manifest hash)
- [ ] Version comparison (semver)
- [ ] Discovery (local, registry)
- [ ] Content-addressed storage (blake3 hash)

## Mocking Strategy
- Mock connector processes (no real subprocess)
- Mock IPC communications (channel-based)
- Inject various failure modes (crash, timeout, malformed)
- MockClock for deterministic timeout testing

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-t77","title":"fcp.jira: Comment and attachment handling","description":"# fcp.jira: Comment and attachment handling (FCP2)

## Goal
Add first-class support for Jira issue **comments** and **attachments**, including safe upload/download flows that preserve FCP2 security invariants.

Comments and attachments are high-leverage for real workflows, but they also carry:
- PII risk
- large binary payload risk
- data exfil risk

## Scope
### Comments
- List comments for an issue (pagination).
- Create/update/delete comments (with visibility rules when supported).
- Convert between:
  - Jira Cloud ADF (Atlassian Document Format)
  - a canonical connector representation (e.g., markdown + structured spans)

### Attachments
- List attachments for an issue (metadata only).
- Download attachment content (streamed; size limits; hash).
- Upload attachment (streamed; content-type allowlist; filename sanitization).
- Delete attachment (dangerous; approval-gated).

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `jira.comments.read`
- `jira.comments.write`
- `jira.attachments.read`
- `jira.attachments.write` (dangerous: data egress into Jira)
- `jira.attachments.delete` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned to Jira base hostname and any attachment host if distinct.
- No secrets in logs.
- **Binary safety**:
  - enforce max download size; abort early
  - enforce max upload size; abort early
  - compute content hash for receipts/audit evidence
- **Taint model**:
  - comment bodies and attachment filenames are tainted inputs
  - do not allow tainted text to trigger dangerous operations without approval

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `jira.comment.list`
  - `jira.comment.create`
  - `jira.comment.update`
  - `jira.comment.delete`
  - `jira.attachment.list`
  - `jira.attachment.download`
  - `jira.attachment.upload`
  - `jira.attachment.delete`
- Streaming download/upload helpers compatible with sandboxed IO.

## Test strategy (MUST)
### Unit/integration (mock-only)
- ADF ↔ markdown conversion golden tests.
- Upload tests:
  - filename normalization
  - content-type allowlist
  - size limits
- Download tests:
  - streaming behavior
  - early abort on oversized content
  - hash correctness

### Compliance
- Any write/delete emits OperationReceipt + AuditEvent with:
  - issue key
  - comment id / attachment id
  - content hash (never raw content)

## Acceptance criteria
- Comments round-trip through conversion without losing critical structure.
- Attachment IO is streaming + bounded (no unbounded buffering).
- Dangerous operations are capability + (policy) approval gated and audited.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-t9nj","title":"FIX: connector_target_from_env_matches_runtime test arch normalization","description":"## Bug Summary

The test `connector_target_from_env_matches_runtime` was failing because it compared `ConnectorTarget::from_env().arch` directly with `std::env::consts::ARCH`.

However, `ConnectorTarget::from_env()` intentionally normalizes architecture names for OCI/Docker compatibility:
- `x86_64` → `amd64`
- `aarch64` → `arm64`

## Fix

Updated the test to expect the normalized arch value by using the same mapping logic that `from_env()` uses.

## Files Changed

- `crates/fcp-registry/src/lib.rs`: Fixed test assertion to account for arch normalization

## Verification

- Full workspace tests pass
- No clippy warnings introduced","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T01:29:43.400900968-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T01:29:59.155461943-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T01:29:59.155461943-05:00","close_reason":"Closed","labels":["registry","testing"]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-thy","title":"fcp.twilio: SMS sending and receiving","description":"# fcp.twilio: SMS sending and receiving (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide core SMS/MMS capabilities:
- send outbound SMS (and MMS when enabled)
- read message status/history
- support inbound SMS via webhook-driven event flow (ingest handled in `flywheel_connectors-ugp`)

## Scope
### Outbound
- Send SMS to a single destination.
- Optional: bulk send with batching + rate limiting.
- Support idempotency keys to prevent duplicate sends.

### Status / history
- Get message details by SID.
- List messages with filters (date range, to/from).

### Media (MMS)
- List/download media attachments (bounded + host pinned).

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `twilio.sms.send`
- `twilio.sms.read`
- `twilio.sms.media.read`

Policy guidance:
- Outbound send is a real-world side effect; many orgs require approval for certain destinations.

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned to Twilio API host(s) and explicit media hosts.
- For sends:
  - receipts + audit events with message SID + to/from hashes (never raw phone numbers in logs)
  - strict log redaction for phone numbers and message bodies
- Taint model:
  - inbound message bodies are tainted by default

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `twilio.sms.send`
  - `twilio.sms.get`
  - `twilio.sms.list`
  - `twilio.sms.media.list`
  - `twilio.sms.media.download`
- Idempotency strategy:
  - stable client idempotency key → dedupe cache/state

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - send request formation
  - idempotency behavior
  - status polling
  - media download size limits
  - secret/PII redaction in logs

## Acceptance criteria
- Duplicate sends are mechanically prevented via idempotency.
- Logs and receipts never leak phone numbers or message bodies.
